content:
Here’s a step‐by‐step check of the smallest primes \(p\) to see whether \(p\) divides
\[
S \;=\;\sum_{k=1}^{2010}9^k \;=\;\frac{9\bigl(9^{2010}-1\bigr)}{8}\,.
\]

1.  \(p=2\).  
    • Numerator \(9(9^{2010}-1)\) has \(v_2=4\) (by LTE), so after dividing by \(8=2^3\) one factor of 2 remains.  
    • Hence \(2\mid S\).

2.  \(p=3\).  
    • Each term \(9^k\) is divisible by 3, so \(3\mid S\).

3.  \(p=5\).  
    • \(9\equiv4\pmod5\), so \(S\equiv\sum_{k=1}^{2010}4^k
               =\sum_{k=1}^{2010}(-1)^k=0\pmod5\).  
    • Hence \(5\mid S\).

4.  \(p=7\).  
    • \(9\equiv2\pmod7\), so 
      \[
        S\equiv\sum_{k=1}^{2010}2^k
         =2^{2011}-2\pmod7.
      \]
    • Since \(\mathrm{ord}_7(2)=3\) and \(2011\equiv1\pmod3\), \(2^{2011}\equiv2\),
      so \(2^{2011}-2\equiv0\).  
    • Hence \(7\mid S\).

5.  \(p=11\).  
    • \(\mathrm{ord}_{11}(9)=5\) and \(2010\equiv0\pmod5\), so \(9^{2010}\equiv1\pmod{11}\).  
    • Thus the numerator \(9(9^{2010}-1)\equiv0\pmod{11}\), so \(11\mid S\).

6.  \(p=13\).  
    • \(\mathrm{ord}_{13}(9)=3\) and \(2010\equiv0\pmod3\), so \(9^{2010}\equiv1\pmod{13}\).  
    • Hence \(13\mid S\).

7.  \(p=17\).  
    • \(\mathrm{ord}_{17}(9)=8\) and \(2011\equiv3\pmod8\), so 
      \(9^{2011}\equiv9^3\equiv15\pmod{17}\).  
    • Then 
      \[
        9^{2011}-9\equiv15-9=6\not\equiv0\pmod{17},
      \]
      so even before dividing by 8, the numerator isn’t 0 mod 17.  
    • Therefore \(17\nmid S\).

Conclusion: the smallest prime that does **not** divide \(S\) is 

\[
\boxed{17}.
\]

Here’s a step‐by‐step check of the smallest primes \(p\) to see whether \(p\) divides
\[
S \;=\;\sum_{k=1}^{2010}9^k \;=\;\frac{9\bigl(9^{2010}-1\bigr)}{8}\,.
\]

1.  \(p=2\).  
    • Numerator \(9(9^{2010}-1)\) has \(v_2=4\) (by LTE), so after dividing by \(8=2^3\) one factor of 2 remains.  
    • Hence \(2\mid S\).

2.  \(p=3\).  
    • Each term \(9^k\) is divisible by 3, so \(3\mid S\).

3.  \(p=5\).  
    • \(9\equiv4\pmod5\), so \(S\equiv\sum_{k=1}^{2010}4^k
               =\sum_{k=1}^{2010}(-1)^k=0\pmod5\).  
    • Hence \(5\mid S\).

4.  \(p=7\).  
    • \(9\equiv2\pmod7\), so 
      \[
        S\equiv\sum_{k=1}^{2010}2^k
         =2^{2011}-2\pmod7.
      \]
    • Since \(\mathrm{ord}_7(2)=3\) and \(2011\equiv1\pmod3\), \(2^{2011}\equiv2\),
      so \(2^{2011}-2\equiv0\).  
    • Hence \(7\mid S\).

5.  \(p=11\).  
    • \(\mathrm{ord}_{11}(9)=5\) and \(2010\equiv0\pmod5\), so \(9^{2010}\equiv1\pmod{11}\).  
    • Thus the numerator \(9(9^{2010}-1)\equiv0\pmod{11}\), so \(11\mid S\).

6.  \(p=13\).  
    • \(\mathrm{ord}_{13}(9)=3\) and \(2010\equiv0\pmod3\), so \(9^{2010}\equiv1\pmod{13}\).  
    • Hence \(13\mid S\).

7.  \(p=17\).  
    • \(\mathrm{ord}_{17}(9)=8\) and \(2011\equiv3\pmod8\), so 
      \(9^{2011}\equiv9^3\equiv15\pmod{17}\).  
    • Then 
      \[
        9^{2011}-9\equiv15-9=6\not\equiv0\pmod{17},
      \]
      so even before dividing by 8, the numerator isn’t 0 mod 17.  
    • Therefore \(17\nmid S\).

Conclusion: the smallest prime that does **not** divide \(S\) is 

\[
\boxed{17}.
\]
